abstract: Higher education across Africa is currently undergoing a period of profound transformation, sparked by a rapid and massive increase in student numbers. This "flash flood" of growth is unique in its scale and speed, bringing with it significant repercussions for university funding, governance, academic quality, and graduate employment. Paradoxically, while the continent has seen record-breaking expansion, the African higher education system remains the least developed in the world, leaving institutions to grapple with the dual burden of increasing access while maintaining rigor. Serving as the introduction to the inaugural issue of the International Journal of African Higher Education, this article synthesizes the current state of the sector. Drawing on the latest research and forthcoming studies, it outlines the critical themes explored in this volume—from the financing conundrum and gender inequities to the challenges of internationalization and the burgeoning demand for graduate education.
